extracted.brandBackground: i9 Sports franchises operate youth amateur sports leagues, camps, tournaments, and clinics.

leadership.name: Ron Shimek
leadership.background: Served as President of i9 Sports since January 2026 and previously President of Mr. Handyman SPV LLC.
leadership.whyItMatters: Directs the overall strategic vision and operations of the franchise system.

leadership.name: Robert Price
leadership.background: CEO of parent company Youth Enrichment Brands since June 2025, formerly President of School of Rock.
leadership.whyItMatters: Oversees the parent portfolio company guiding the brand's high-level growth.

leadership.name: Hali Hill
leadership.background: General Counsel for YEB since May 2024, previously Deputy General Counsel of ServiceMaster Opco Holdings.
leadership.whyItMatters: Manages legal compliance and regulatory affairs for the franchise network.

leadership.name: Elliot Schiffer
leadership.background: Chief Development Officer since April 2024, previously Interim CEO of Escalante's Comida Fina.
leadership.whyItMatters: Leads franchise development, recruitment, and system expansion.

operationalRisks.title: Out-of-State Dispute Resolution
operationalRisks.description: The franchise agreement requires you to resolve disputes with the franchisor by mediation only in Florida.

operationalRisks.title: Minimum Performance Standards
operationalRisks.description: Must meet or exceed a minimum annual player registration revenue of $80,000 after one year.

extracted.conceptRationale: i9 Sports provides youth sports leagues, camps, and clinics, which are experiential recreational activities.
extracted.staffingRationale: Leagues and camps are run on-site at rented fields/venues using part-time coordinators, coaches, and referees.

riskReasons: A high-severity operational tripwire is disclosed — see the tripwires section.
notes: Average rent not found; fixed costs may be understated.

underwriting.assessment: $250,000 liquid covers the $64,900 mid-point build-out without financing, though that leaves little working-capital cushion. At mid-cohort revenue, modeled cash flow after debt service is about $25,879/mo before payroll and maintenance — a thin margin of error.

insights.conceptRationale: i9 Sports provides youth sports leagues, camps, and clinics, which are experiential recreational activities.

benchmark.label: Experiential / entertainment venue
benchmark.dominantRisk: Fixed-cost coverage and utilization — big-box rent and equipment R&M against variable foot traffic. Slow weeks hurt more than COGS.
benchmark.rampNote: 6–18 months to a mature traffic/booking base; year-one runs well under the Item 19 average.
considerations: COGS is modest; the real costs are labor (~22–30%), big-footprint rent, and equipment maintenance.
Ask about utilization/occupancy rates and seasonality at Item 20 units — the model lives or dies on weekday/off-peak traffic.
Confirm equipment R&M and replacement reserves; experiential gear wears and isn't in the pro forma.

crossCheck.message: No operating-EBITDA margin is disclosed in Item 19. The 12–22% band below is an industry estimate for this concept — validate it against real franchisee P&Ls (Item 20) before relying on any margin.

insights.staffingNote: Flagged as an lightly staffed model from the FDD, so labor is adjusted down to 11–18% — the 22–30% category band assumes a staffed venue. Confirm actual staffing with Item 20 franchisees.

buildup.label: − Occupancy / rent (not disclosed)
buildup.note: Item 7 disclosed no rent figure — benchmark occupancy applied so the line above isn't overstated

buildup.label: − Labor
buildup.note: lightly staffed · ≈ 1–1.6 FTE at ~$20/hr fully loaded

assumptions.detail: Classified as “Experiential / entertainment venue” from the FDD (Item 1 / brand description).
Read as lightly staffed from the FDD, which lowers the labor band applied below.
Item 7 disclosed no rent — occupancy estimated at the 10–18% category band.
Estimated at the 8–18% category band — COGS is never disclosed in an FDD.
Estimated at the 11–18% lightly staffed band — labor is never disclosed in an FDD.
Utilities, insurance, and R&M estimated at 6–10% (category catch-all).
Our calculation — the disclosed top line minus the benchmarked costs above. Not a figure stated in the FDD.

insights.consultCtaPitch: These are category benchmarks applied to the disclosed top line — your unit's real costs will differ, and that difference is the conversation. Want them pressure-tested against actual franchisee P&Ls for your territory?
insights.asOf: 2026 — general industry benchmarks, refine against real franchisee P&Ls
insights.disclaimer: Industry benchmark ranges for your own budgeting and Item 20 questions — NOT a projection of this franchise's results, and not investment advice.

financialCondition.headline: The franchisor flagged its own financial condition as a risk. The figures we extracted look comparatively stable, so confirm directly against the most recent statements.
body: Why it matters to you: field support, training, technology, and marketing come out of the franchisor's budget. If outside funding slows, those are the first things to get cut. There is no parent guarantee of performance in this FDD, so Youth Enrichment Brands, LLC is not contractually obligated to keep funding the franchisor or backing its commitments to you.

Verify before you commit: ask for the most recent interim (unaudited) financials and the current-year funding plan; ask whether the parent will sign a guarantee of performance; and cross-check Item 20 — unit closures, transfers, and terminations are the operational tell that corroborates financial stress.
aggravators: no parent guarantee backing the franchisor's obligations to you
mitigants: the auditor issued a clean (unmodified) opinion and did not raise going-concern doubt
financialCondition.evidenceNote: Flagged on the FDD's "Special Risks" page and grounded in the Item 21 / Exhibit F audited financials. Figures are extracted; all ratios and grading are computed. Confirm against the source statements and a CPA before relying on them.
